original_abstract: To address the issues of hallucination and factual inaccuracy in large language models (LMs), we propose a novel framework for **Forward-Looking Active REtrieval augmented generation (FLARE)**. While standard retrieval-augmented LMs typically retrieve information only once based on the initial user prompt, this "retrieve-and-generate" bottleneck is often insufficient for long-form generation where information needs evolve as the text progresses. FLARE overcomes this by actively deciding when and what to retrieve throughout the generation process. Specifically, the system monitors the confidence of the LM; if the model generates a temporary sentence containing low-probability tokens, FLARE treats that sentence as a placeholder for future intent and uses it as a query to retrieve relevant external documents. The model then regenerates the sentence based on the fetched knowledge. We evaluate FLARE across four complex, long-form knowledge-intensive tasks, including multi-hop reasoning, open-domain summarization, and long-form question answering. Our results demonstrate that FLARE consistently outperforms or remains competitive with existing single-time and multi-time retrieval baselines, highlighting its effectiveness in maintaining factual atttribution in extended text generation.
humanized_abstract: In this work, we introduce a new framework referred to as Forward-Looking Active REtrieval augmented generation (FLARE). The common retrieve-and-generate approach of retrieval-augmented LMs only retrieves information based on the input query for one time, which is insufficient for long-form generation due to the evolving information needed for long texts. Currently, LMs have no mechanism to determine when and what information should be retrieved during generation. For this reason, we propose a model that can dynamically decide when and what to retrieve during the generation process in order to prevent hallucinations and maintain factual accuracy. The basic idea is to monitor the output probability distribution of the LM and treat any temporary generated sentence that contains rare tokens as a placeholder for future intention in the context of the input query. The model then treats the generated sentence as a query to search a document corpus for relevant documents, and generates the sentence again based on the searched knowledge. We experiment our proposed model with four knowledge-intensive tasks, including multi-hop reasoning, open-domain summarization, and long-form question answering, and show that FLARE significantly outperforms the baselines (i.e single-time and multi-time retrieval) for all tasks.
